The VP of Partnerships posting is a more senior, structurally different role; the cover letter must address the prior rejection directly.

watch_list.why: Israeli startup combining satellite data, climate models, and AI for agricultural supply chain resilience. Land-based value chains. Direct EO application with climate lens.
watch_list.roast: Tel Aviv outfit calling itself a copilot for resilient land-based value chains, which is consultant for we help farms not die when the weather goes sideways. They layer satellite data over climate models and call it AI insights, which is half the EO pitch decks you saw at KSAT and all the ones you will see if you take this job. The product is real, the use case is obvious, the market is every agribusiness pretending sustainable sourcing is more than a website paragraph. You have spent a decade making satellite data legible to buyers who do not want to become remote sensing experts. Marvin is selling the exact same translation problem in a different vertical. You know the NDVI is not the insight, the insight is knowing which supply chain manager has budget authority and why this quarter their bonus depends on proving they have visibility into upstream risk. You closed Planet at 95 percent off commercial rates by understanding who actually signs. Marvin will want someone who has done it before and who will not waste six months trying to sell the technology instead of the outcome. The risk is Israel-based in 2026, which complicates US federal and some commercial accounts, but if they are smart they have thought about that already. If they have not, you will spend the first 90 days explaining it.

watch_list.why: Extreme weather forecasting using radar and satellite. Climate adaptation tech, early warning systems. EO backbone with commercial and gov customers.
watch_list.roast: Climavision builds radar and satellite-based weather forecasting for the people who cannot afford to be wrong about the weather, which in 2026 is basically everyone with a supply chain, a power grid, or a field. Extreme weather is expensive and getting more expensive, and the NOAA forecast is not granular enough to tell you whether to reroute the truck or call off the linemen. Climavision is selling speed and accuracy, which matters to utilities, insurance, shipping, and emergency planning. Paul sold ExxonMobil and Shell on proactive monitoring by showing them what it cost when they did not know. Climavision has the same pitch but the buyer is broader and the urgency is higher because the weather is not a compliance risk, it is an operational risk that shows up every quarter. The companies that win in climate adaptation are the ones that close the boring contract with the Midwestern utility in month four, not the ones that raise another round on the promise of a better model. If Climavision has revenue and a repeatable sales motion, they are worth a conversation. If they are still selling the vision, Paul should wait.

watch_list.why: Satellite imagery and ML to verify forest carbon projects. Nature-based carbon market infrastructure. YC-backed, major customers (Amazon, Airbnb, Netflix). Direct overlap with GFW Planet deal skillset and carbon market framing.
watch_list.roast: San Francisco forest carbon verification play that uses satellite imagery and machine learning to tell buyers whether the trees they paid for are actually standing or whether someone cut them down and planted corn. The entire nature-based carbon market only works if the credits are real, and Pachama has decided to be the referee, which is a terrible job with exceptional leverage. They have Amazon, Airbnb, and Netflix as customers, which means they have figured out how to sell to corporate sustainability teams with real budgets, and they are backed by Bill Gates and Chris Sacca, which means the cap table has more climate cred than most climate conferences. You built the Planet three-party deal and you spent two years explaining to insurance buyers and conservation funders why GFW-derived data products are not the same as the open-access platform. Pachama is the same structural tension packaged differently. The free satellite data is everywhere, the value is in the methodology, the validation, and the willingness to stake your reputation on the outputs. You know this pattern. You also know the carbon market is a minefield of credibility problems, which means Pachama spends half its time defending its methodology and the other half explaining why it is not just Verra with better marketing. If you go in, you will spend the first month learning why every objection you expect is wrong and the second month realizing some of them are not.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based dMRV for rice methane projects, Gold Standard pilot, ag carbon space aligns with Planet/GFW EO background
watch_list.roast: Paris-based outfit running the Gold Standard's first satellite-verified rice carbon project, which sounds impressive until you remember that rice paddies have been flooding and draining for five thousand years without anyone needing a Sentinel-2 feed and a machine learning model to confirm it happened. They're pitching digital MRV as the solution to scaling farmer-led climate finance, which is consultant for 'we will sell you software so you can spend less time arguing with auditors and more time explaining to a Bengali smallholder why this year's carbon credit payment is three months late because the cloud cover was bad in July.' The tech works. The pilot makes sense. The Gold Standard partnership is real. But you have seen this movie before at GFW, where the distance between a functioning MRV platform and a functioning revenue model is the length of the Tromso winter, and somebody still has to convince the buyer that the credit is worth more than the trouble. If they can make the unit economics work at paddy scale, they have built something no one else has. If they cannot, they have built a very expensive dashboard for an NGO that runs out of grant money in 2027. You would spend the first six months finding out which one it is, and you are extremely good at that.

watch_list.why: Boston-based, field spectroscopy for soil carbon, bridges EO/ground truth gap, solves expensive lab analysis problem in carbon markets
watch_list.roast: Boston outfit that decided the way to fix soil carbon MRV was to stop pretending you can do it all from space and just build a better shovel. Their proprietary probe uses spectroscopy to measure soil carbon content instantly in the field, no lab, no six-week turnaround, no $50 per sample cost structure that makes soil carbon projects uneconomical at any scale a farmer would recognize. It is a hardware play in a software-obsessed market, which means they either have something genuinely defensible or they have a very expensive field instrument that breaks when you drop it in Iowa mud. The thesis is sound: soil carbon is the problem child of carbon markets because measurement is brutal and satellite-based proxies are just fancy guesses with error bars nobody wants to admit in the methodology. If Yard Stick can cut MRV costs by two-thirds and make the data credible enough that Verra and Gold Standard accept it without a fight, they have removed the primary constraint on a multi-billion-dollar market that currently does not function. If they cannot, they have built a science project. You would spend the first three months figuring out whether their spectroscopy actually works across soil types or whether it only works in the test plots they show investors, and that is a question you are mean enough to ask in month one instead of month eighteen when the enterprise customer realizes the data does not match the lab and the contract goes sideways. They need someone who has sold technical credibility to skeptical buyers with sharp pencils, and you sold Shell a polar monitoring service they did not think they needed until you made the alternative feel riskier than writing the check.

watch_list.why: Satellite-powered digital MRV platform, third-party validated, farm/field/forest scale carbon monitoring, independent platform with no third-party incentives
watch_list.roast: CarbonSpace bills itself as independent and scientifically rigorous, which in the carbon MRV world means they watched Pachama and Sylvera raise nine figures and decided somebody needs to be the grownup in the room. Their pitch is peer-reviewed algorithms and zero perverse incentives, which is either refreshingly honest or a polite way of saying they have no idea how to monetize this yet. You spent a decade negotiating Planet imagery deals and ASN subsea cable partnerships where the carbon story was adjacent, not central. Here it is central, and the buyer is an ESG compliance officer at a reinsurer who needs audit-proof documentation before the CFO finds out what the premium increase looks like. CarbonSpace is betting the voluntary carbon market will grow a spine and demand real verification. You have spent your entire career finding the moment when a client realizes the status quo is riskier than the alternative. If that moment arrives for soil carbon the way it did for NOPSEMA offshore monitoring, CarbonSpace is the kind of platform that becomes the de facto standard. If it does not, they are a science project with a website. The question is whether you want to be in the room when that tipping point either happens or does not. Remote first, decent chance they need someone who has actually sold satellite-derived data products to someone other than a grant officer.

watch_list.why: US-built electric cargo vessels, MIT Ocean Engineering pedigree, $100M+ in sales, deploying early 2026, maritime innovation background, exactly Paul's domain
watch_list.roast: Voltic is building battery and solar powered cargo ships and claiming they are four times more profitable than diesel, which either means they found the naval architecture equivalent of cold fusion or their Excel model has some very optimistic assumptions about charging infrastructure in Philadelphia and retrofit timelines in Oakland. They spun out of MIT Ocean Engineering, raised money from the largest maritime VC in the world, and have over $100 million in signed sales, so somebody believes the physics work. The founding team previously started two shipping lines, which means they know how to move containers and also know how many ways a ship can lose money. You sold oil spill monitoring to Shell and ExxonMobil, you built the SkyTruth to GFW ecosystem over a decade, you closed the Planet Labs coastal monitoring deal, and you structured the ASN subsea cable partnership. Voltic is trying to electrify the dirtiest part of the global supply chain, and their early customers are either visionary or desperate. The commercial question is whether shipping companies will pay a green premium or whether this only works with IRA subsidies and California port incentives that disappear in 2027. If the unit economics are real, this is a category-defining company. If they are not, it is a very expensive science experiment that runs out of runway somewhere between the second ship and the third. Either way, you would be selling into the exact same maritime domain awareness and logistics buyers you have spent fifteen years cultivating, except this time the product is the ship instead of the imagery of the ship. That is either a natural adjacency or a costly distraction, depending on whether you want to sell hardware or data.

watch_list.why: Stuttgart-based, €5M seed March 2026, first satellite 2027. Methane monitoring from space with SWIR+micro-LIDAR at 2x accuracy vs existing systems. ESA-backed. Fits EO+climate+MRV commercial licensing experience perfectly.
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade at KSAT selling oil majors on proactive spill monitoring when they were pretending to care about their social license, and now here comes AIRMO out of Stuttgart with a rocket scientist founder and a satellite designed to catch methane leaks the energy industry has been hemorrhaging for profit. They raised €5 million in March from Ananda and kopa ventures to fly SWIR imaging with micro-LIDAR miniaturized onto a smallsat, which sounds like vaporware until you see ESA backing and airborne systems already flying commercial contracts in Europe and Central Asia. The pitch is that 70 percent of methane emissions go undetected, costing the energy sector $100 billion a year, $30 billion of that in straight revenue loss, which means there are CFOs at oil majors and pipeline operators who will pay real money if the data is defensible and the measurement is repeatable and you can walk them through how it works without making them feel stupid. That is the same conversation you had with Shell in The Hague in 2012 about why they should pay KSAT to watch their platforms when MDA had the incumbent contract. AIRMO is pre-revenue, probably six months from figuring out what enterprise sales actually means, and you have sold satellite-derived environmental monitoring to the exact verticals they are about to discover. The founder is technical, the investors are impact-first, and they will need someone who can explain to a procurement officer at TotalEnergies why continuous orbital monitoring creates regulatory and reputational value even when the law does not require it yet. Also they are in Germany, which you lived next door to for a decade, and they will absolutely botch the North American market entry unless someone who has actually closed energy contracts gets in early. This is the KSAT pitch all over again but with methane and the regulatory pressure is higher now than it was in 2015.

watch_list.why: California-based, $95M state contract signed March 2026 for real-time emissions enforcement. Satellite constellation detecting methane and CO2 point sources. Government and corporate licensing revenue model.
watch_list.roast: Carbon Mapper just signed a $95 million contract with the State of California for real-time emissions enforcement, which means they went from being a satellite company to being the enforcement arm of a regulatory regime, and that should make every oil and gas operator and landfill manager and wastewater facility in the state very nervous and very willing to pay for early access to the same data the state is using to write citations. You know this pattern. It is the pilot-to-precedent engine you ran at KSAT when you helped draft oil spill legislation in Nigeria and got KSAT language into AMSA RFPs in Australia. Carbon Mapper is flying purpose-built satellites to detect methane and CO2 point sources at facility scale, and the California contract proves the regulatory demand is real and someone is willing to pay government rates for continuous monitoring. The company is a partnership that spun out of JPL and university researchers, which means strong technical credibility and probably weak commercial instincts, and they are about to get flooded with inbound interest from every industrial emitter who wants to know what Carbon Mapper can see before the regulators do. They need someone who understands how to sell proactive monitoring to companies that do not want to be monitored, who can structure licensing deals with enterprises that have compliance and PR concerns baked into every conversation, and who knows how to price surveillance when the customer views it as insurance against regulatory penalties they have not budgeted for yet. The California contract is proof of concept for the regulatory market. The next $95 million comes from Chevron and Equinor and ConocoPhillips buying the same data stream under a commercial license so they can fix leaks before the state publishes them. You have sold this exact service. They need you six months ago.

watch_list.why: Announced Merlin constellation for daily one-meter multi-spectral remapping. Sub-daily revisit competing with Planet on coverage. Commercial imagery at scale.
watch_list.roast: Satellogic just announced Merlin, their new one-meter daily remap play, which means they finally looked at Planet's pricing and thought, we can do that cheaper. You spent a decade at KSAT selling monitoring contracts to people who needed proof the oil slick happened yesterday, not last week, so you know exactly what daily revisit unlocks and what it costs when the competitor launches faster than you close. Satellogic has been the plucky South American underdog since 2010, still trying to prove they belong in the room with Maxar and Planet, still pitching ten spectral bands like it is a personality. The good news is they are well funded, the constellation is real, and they have actual customers who pay. The bad news is commercial EO is a knife fight and you would be walking in as the partnerships guy tasked with making Merlin feel differentiated when everyone already has a Planet contract and a Maxar backup. But if you want to sell imagery where the margin lives in frequency not resolution, and you miss the days when you had to explain why a client should pay for monitoring they did not think they needed yet, this is that fight all over again. Just with more Slack and fewer oil majors.

watch_list.why: Operating HiVE constellation for land surface temperature. Agriculture, utilities, infrastructure use cases. Thermal data gap Planet and Maxar do not fill.
watch_list.roast: constellr is the German thermal infrared play that everyone in ag tech and utilities has heard about but nobody has actually bought yet, which is the story of every climate dataset until somebody's insurance premium doubles and suddenly thermal anomaly detection stops being a nice to have. You sold EO services into offshore energy when the business case was build it and maybe they will pay, so you understand the gap between a working sensor and a working revenue model. constellr has the HiVE constellation flying, they have NanoAvionics building the buses, and they are generating actual land surface temperature data that matters for crop stress, power grid resilience, and construction planning. The problem is thermal IR is expensive to build, hard to sell, and the customers who need it most are municipalities and agricultural co-ops who move at the speed of procurement hell. If you join, you are the first commercial hire walking into a team of German engineers who have spent five years perfecting the sensor and approximately eight minutes thinking about how to make a farmer in Iowa pay for it. But if you want to sell a dataset that genuinely does not exist anywhere else at scale, and you still remember what it felt like to walk into Shell in 2012 with a monitoring capability nobody asked for and walk out with a contract, constellr is that same bet with worse margins and better science.

watch_list.why: Satellite-based energy and environmental analytics. Just acquired by Energy Aspects for market intelligence integration. Methane, oil storage, shipping tracking.
watch_list.roast: Kayrros is the French satellite analytics firm that spent a decade building the best methane and energy infrastructure monitoring product nobody in oil and gas wanted to admit they needed, and then got acquired by Energy Aspects in March 2026 because apparently the only way to monetize climate data is to bundle it with the commodity intelligence the traders already pay for. You have spent your entire career trying to sell environmental monitoring to people who would rather not know, so you will recognize the Kayrros model immediately. They built a machine learning platform that detects methane plumes, tracks floating oil storage, and estimates production at refineries nobody wants monitored, all from satellite imagery they do not own, which is the right business model if you can survive long enough for regulation to make your product mandatory. The Energy Aspects acquisition is either a rescue or a victory lap depending on whether you think Kayrros was about to run out of runway or had already won. Either way, you would be walking into a post-acquisition integration where the French engineers who built the algos now report to London market analysts who think satellite data is a feature not a product. If you want to sell methane monitoring as a compliance tool in the two-year window before the EPA Endangerment Finding gets reinstated, and you do not mind that your beautiful environmental dataset is now a widget inside somebody else's commodity terminal, Kayrros is available. Just maybe check if they still have a partnerships function after the acquisition closed.

watch_list.why: UK thermal imaging satellite constellation, just IPO'd May 2026 at $26/share, commercial traction in climate risk and infrastructure monitoring
watch_list.roast: SatVu just priced their IPO at the top of the range and you are supposed to believe thermal infrared from LEO is finally ready for commercial prime time after two decades of false starts. They are selling the same dream MDA sold when you were at KSAT, which is that someone will pay real money to watch pipelines leak at night. The difference is they actually launched satellites and they actually found buyers who care when a data center is about to melt or a petrochemical plant is cooking itself into an insurance claim. You spent a decade explaining to oil majors why they should pay for SAR. You know exactly how hard it is to get an asset owner to admit they need better monitoring than they already pretend to have. SatVu has UK government anchor revenue, which means they navigated the same credibility gap you did with EMSA and AMSA but in a market segment where Maxar gave up. If they survive the next eighteen months without a second dilutive round they will own a category. If they do not, you will have watched the thermal EO dream die in public again. Either way it is worth the conversation because they are doing the KSAT thing but with a constellation and a Nasdaq ticker.

watch_list.why: Swedish onboard processing and edge compute for EO satellites, panel presence at SmallSat Europe 2026, selling deployed product not demos
watch_list.roast: Unibap showed up at SmallSat Europe this week with actual deployed onboard AI instead of another white paper about how onboard AI will someday be deployed, which in the European space conference circuit is roughly equivalent to showing up with a unicorn. Johan Åman sat on the latency arbitrage panel and made the quiet observation that the commercial near term case for edge compute is smaller and sharper than the orbital data center fever dream, which means someone at Unibap has actually tried to sell this and lived to revise the pitch. You lived through the KSAT value-added services build when everyone wanted to add compute at the edge and nobody wanted to pay for the compute at the edge. Unibap is doing that again but in orbit, which is harder and more expensive and involves convincing satellite operators to let you put your code on their spacecraft. The Swedish government is probably buying some of this. The real question is whether a non-government EO customer will pay to run inference at 500 kilometers instead of waiting six hours to downlink the raster and run it in AWS. If the answer is yes Unibap owns a structural advantage in latency-sensitive applications. If the answer is no they have built very expensive technology for a market that does not exist yet. You can recognize that conversation in your sleep.

watch_list.why: AI-driven satellite tasking and mission planning, CEO Guy de Carufel speaking on AI in space defense at SmallSat Europe, defense procurement angle
watch_list.roast: Cognitive Space is selling AI that decides which satellite looks at what and when, which is the operational planning problem that made you want to burn your laptop at KSAT every time a customer called with a same-day tasking request during a polar weather window. Guy de Carufel stood on the defense stage at SmallSat Europe this week and said out loud that when missiles are inbound you do not have time to call a meeting and get consensus, which is the kind of talk that gets European procurement people very uncomfortable and American defense primes very interested. The company is building decision automation for multi-sensor constellations in contested environments, which is a polite way of saying they are teaching satellites to task themselves when humans are too slow. You know from Tromso that coordinating even three satellites across four ground stations with two weather systems and one grumpy customer is a Tetris problem that breaks people. Cognitive Space is trying to make the machine solve it in real time and then sell that capability to governments who are not allowed to let the machine make kinetic decisions but are quietly admitting they have no other option. The business model works if defense buyers believe the autonomy or it does not work at all. There is no middle outcome here. You have sold into that risk calculus before and you know exactly how long the contract cycle is and exactly how many times the requirement will change. But if they close DOD or ESA revenue in 2026 they become the reference architecture for autonomous space operations and you were in the room early.

watch_list.why: Bengaluru and El Segundo. Hyperspectral satellite imagery. Strong commercial traction. Fits Paul's EO data sourcing and commercial licensing background.
watch_list.roast: Hyperspectral is the new SAR. Everyone wants it, nobody knows what to charge for it, and half the buyers think multispectral is good enough until a consultant convinces them otherwise. Pixxel is betting that 150+ spectral bands will unlock use cases invisible to Planet's RGB world, which is probably true, but the commercial question is whether those use cases come with budget authority or just PowerPoint enthusiasm. Paul spent a decade at KSAT explaining why oil companies should pay for proactive monitoring instead of waiting for the spill. Hyperspectral has the same problem but worse, the value proposition requires the buyer to imagine a future state they have never experienced. Pixxel has raised serious money and launched actual satellites, so they are past the vaporware stage, but they are still in the phase where the technology is ahead of the market's ability to articulate the RFP. If Paul can help them find the buyers who will pay before the use case is fully proven, this is the kind of builder stage commercial role he has owned twice before. Dual headquarters in India and California means time zones are a nightmare but the team has already navigated that. The risk is that hyperspectral becomes a feature someone else bundles rather than a standalone business. The opportunity is that Pixxel is early enough and funded enough to define the category before the aggregators swallow it.

watch_list.why: Australian. Onboard AI processing for EO satellites. Reduces bandwidth, increases speed. Fits Paul's satellite operations and commercial data background.
watch_list.roast: Spiral Blue sells the thing every satellite operator wishes they had built five years ago, an onboard computer that processes imagery in orbit so you downlink answers instead of terabytes. The value proposition is clean, bandwidth is expensive, latency is a killer, and most satellite data gets analyzed never or late. Spiral Blue's pitch is do the AI on the bird, send the insight, skip the pipeline. It is a good idea. The problem is that it requires satellite operators to trust a startup's hardware in an environment where a software bug means you bricked a 50 million dollar asset that you cannot reboot by unplugging it. Paul has been on both sides of this, he sold monitoring services to oil majors who wanted proof it works before they pay, and he bought satellite data at GFW where he had to explain to engineers why we could not wait six months for perfect imagery. Spiral Blue is in the hard part now, they have a working product and some pilot customers but they need the reference accounts that make the next twenty sales easier. The opportunity for Paul is that this is a commercial partnership sale, not a transactional close. Spiral Blue's customers are satellite operators and downstream data buyers, which is exactly the ecosystem Paul has lived in since Tromso. The company is Australian, which means remote first is native and time zones with Taos are survivable. The risk is that the big constellation operators build this in house and Spiral Blue ends up with a product the market wants but nobody buys because vertically integrated players do not outsource the edge.

watch_list.category: Passive Microwave Sensing / Soil Moisture EO
watch_list.why: Dutch. Global satellite-based soil moisture and vegetation data. Agriculture, insurance, commodities buyers. Fits Paul's data licensing and commercial vertical-building experience.
watch_list.roast: VanderSat has been flying under the radar, pun intended, doing passive microwave sensing for soil moisture and vegetation while everyone else chases optical and SAR. The company has real customers in agriculture, insurance, and commodities, which are the three verticals where satellite data companies go to find out if their technology is a product or a science project. Soil moisture is one of those variables that matters enormously to people who know it matters and is invisible to everyone else. Paul built the GFW commercial licensing program by identifying where a free platform needed structured commercial relationships, VanderSat has the opposite problem, they have a commercial product and need to convince buyers that passive microwave data is worth paying for when optical imagery is everywhere and free adjacent. The company has been around long enough to survive the trough, which means they have revenue and a business model that works, but they are in the growth phase where they need someone who can articulate why soil moisture data is not just nice to have but a genuine competitive advantage for the buyer. Paul has done this twice, once convincing oil majors to pay KSAT for monitoring they did not think they needed, once convincing insurance companies to pay GFW for vessel data when AIS was supposed to be free. VanderSat is Dutch, which means the company is probably under capitalized by Silicon Valley standards but over engineered by everyone else's, and the culture will be direct in a way that Paul will find refreshing after too many ATS systems that reject him with inspirational messages. The risk is that VanderSat gets acquired by a larger geospatial player and the commercial role evaporates. The opportunity is that they are at the stage where the next fifty customers look like the first fifty and Paul has built that playbook twice.

watch_list.why: Madrid-based, $130M Series B (April 2026), AI-optimized satellite constellation for real-time geospatial data, L3Harris manufacturing partnership. Paul's EO data licensing and multi-stakeholder deal architecture experience is direct fit.
watch_list.roast: Spanish startup that just closed one-thirty million to build AI-native satellites and you are somehow supposed to believe they cracked the unit economics problem that murdered half the EO sector in the last eighteen months. They paired with L3Harris to build the sensors which means someone in Madrid convinced a Florida defense contractor that their architecture is worth betting ruggedized hardware on. The pitch is on-board inference to cut latency and the customer list sounds like every other pitch deck you saw at CommEO last year but the money is real and the timing is sharp. You spent a decade at KSAT explaining to oil majors why they should pay for something they did not know they needed and then you did it again at GFW selling licenses on top of a free platform. Xoople is selling intelligence not imagery and they are doing it in a market where half the buyers still think Planet is expensive. If they survive the next funding winter they will need someone who knows how to build commercial arguments where the technology is two steps ahead of the customer's brain. You have done this twice. They have one-thirty million and a hardware partner and no idea how to sell to the enterprise buyers who are still figuring out what derived data products even are. Also they are in Madrid which is not Tromso but it is not San Francisco either and you already proved you can work in Europe without losing your mind.

watch_list.why: Zurich-based, $11.8M Series A, building Earth Intelligence Platform using satellite data and AI to simulate atmospheric physics. Climate risk modeling customer base overlaps Paul's insurance vertical work (AXA Climate, British Marine/QBE).
watch_list.roast: Swiss startup that raised eleven million euros to build an AI model that learns physics from satellite data because apparently numerical weather prediction is not complicated enough already. They integrate optical sensors, radiometers, radars, and GNSS radio occultation which sounds like they are ingesting every feed on the market and hoping the model figures out what to do with it. The climate tech angle is real and the insurance vertical is live which means someone at AXA or Swiss Re is taking their calls but the question is whether they are selling forecasts or selling the idea of better forecasts. You closed AXA Climate and British Marine QBE deals at GFW by finding the moment when the status quo felt riskier than the pilot and that exact skill is what Jua needs if they are going to convince parametric insurance underwriters to trust a black box model over fifty years of actuarial tables. They are based in Zurich which is the world capital of people who do not take risks they cannot quantify and you are the person who taught conservative buyers how to buy things they did not know existed. The team is technical and the funding is adequate and the market timing is good if you believe extreme weather is going to keep breaking underwriting models. The hard part is not the science, it is making the commercial argument stick when the buyer's entire career depends on not being wrong. You have done that in offshore energy, in fisheries surveillance, in maritime risk. Do it again but this time the customer already knows they have a problem.

watch_list.category: Synthetic Aperture Radar / Middle East EO
watch_list.why: Saudi Arabia-based, $2.6M seed (Tonomus/Aramco VC), building SAR constellation for MENA region (deforestation, border security, disasters, oil leakage). Sovereign EO play with regional focus, fits Paul's government/commercial deal-building and oil/gas monitoring background.
watch_list.roast: Saudi-backed SAR startup that closed two point six million from Tonomus and Aramco Ventures to build the ArabiaEye constellation because apparently the Middle East decided it wants its own satellites now and SARsatX is the chosen vehicle. The pitch is SAR plus optical fusion for the MENA region with use cases that read like a KSAT proposal from 2014 when you were flying to Perth and Houston explaining why oil majors should pay for proactive monitoring. The sovereignty angle is real and the funding sources tell you exactly who the customer is going to be but the question is whether they are building a commercial business or a subsidy recycling program for Vision 2030. You spent years in the North Sea and offshore Australia getting ENI and Woodside to move NOPSEMA toward requiring remote sensing in environmental plans and that playbook works if the regulatory pressure is genuine. Saudi has the budget and the strategic intent but you would be selling into a market where the government is the customer, the investor, and the regulator at the same time which is either the easiest deal structure you ever touched or the most politically suffocating. They are Seraphim alumni which means someone in London thought the business model was credible and the MENA market is underserved and growing fast but you already passed on UP42 because you did not want to explain Saudi ownership to North American enterprise customers. This is the inverse problem but the question is the same which is whether you want to spend the next three years navigating Riyadh instead of Taos.

watch_list.why: Vertical specialist, own constellation, analytics-first model, mentioned alongside ICEYE/Planet in 2026 EO industry roundup
watch_list.roast: Calgary builds hyperspectral satellites promising to see what nobody else can see, which is basically the EO industry in a single sentence. You spent a decade at KSAT selling the idea that someone should pay for what free Sentinel data almost delivers, so you understand exactly what Wyvern is up against. They raised money, they launched hardware, and now comes the hard part where a corporate sustainability officer has to explain to her CFO why 100 spectral bands matter when three bands are free. The technical capability is real. The customer education timeline is long. You have done this movie before, including the part where the pilot works, the customer loves the insights, and procurement says we will think about it and circle back next quarter. But hyperspectral is not vaporware anymore, and if someone is going to make the commercial case stick it will be a team that understands the gap between spectrum and spreadsheet. You closed Shell on a 2.5 year cycle selling oil spill monitoring. Wyvern is selling molecules you cannot see to buyers who are not sure they need them yet. Same game, higher resolution, narrower margin for error. Check the careers page and decide if you want to build the business case one pharma supply chain at a time or wait until someone else proves the market exists.

watch_list.why: US-based, 15cm resolution, VLEO at 250km, defense/intel buyer base, proprietary propulsion for drag mitigation
watch_list.roast: San Francisco decided that flying satellites at half the altitude of everyone else would deliver twice the resolution, which is the kind of engineering logic that works until you hit the atmosphere at 17,000 mph and your orbit decays in three months. EOI Space patented a propulsion system to solve the drag problem, raised the money, and is now in the phase where they have to prove the tech works and someone will pay for 15cm imagery when 30cm is already available and mostly good enough. You have sold Earth observation data to oil majors, governments, and NGOs who all asked the same question at some point, which is why this costs so much. The answer at KSAT was always because physics, and the answer at EOI is the same except the physics are harder and the customer is either DoD or a three-letter agency with a black budget. They are hiring across the board, which means they are scaling to launch, and VLEO is not a fantasy anymore even if the unit economics are still speculative. The company has AFRL partnerships, which is the Space Force putting chips on the table, and that matters more than customer slide decks. This is a builder role at a hard-tech company solving actual propulsion and thermal problems, not a SaaS play with a satellite wrapper. If you want to sell to the commercial market you will spend three years explaining why sub-meter matters. If you want to sell to the government you will spend three years getting clearance and learning RFP compliance. But the imagery will be the best anyone outside of NRO has ever flown commercially, and that counts for something when the entire industry is converging toward higher resolution and lower altitude. The question is whether you want to build the market or wait until someone else proves it.

watch_list.why: EU-based, farm-scale MRV for soil carbon, addressing the measurement gap in ag carbon markets, 4.5M hectares monitored
watch_list.roast: Copenhagen is trying to convince European farmers that soil carbon is worth tracking and commodity buyers that the resulting credits are worth buying, which is approximately as hard as it sounds. Agreena combines remote sensing with soil sampling and machine learning to quantify carbon sequestration in a carbon pool that is invisible, highly variable, and moves around depending on rainfall and what the farmer did last Tuesday. You built a commercial licensing function on top of a free platform at GFW, so you know exactly what it feels like to sell something people think should be free or are not sure they need yet. Agreena has 77 million in funding and 4.5 million hectares under monitoring, which means they survived the 2025 voluntary carbon market stagnation that killed Nori and a dozen other soil carbon startups. They are one of the survivors, which in this market means they either have product-market fit or very patient capital or both. The regulatory tailwind is real, EUDR and EU Methane Regulation are creating demand for verified ag data, and the insurance and food company channels are starting to move. But soil carbon MRV is still a translation problem. You have to sell the science to the farmer, the financial case to the off-taker, and the methodology to the auditor, and all three conversations happen in different languages. This is the kind of complex stakeholder sale you did at KSAT when you were moving NOPSEMA toward requiring proactive monitoring, except the regulator has not mandated it yet and the pilot customers are still figuring out if the data is decision-useful. If you like the idea of building a carbon market from scratch in a category where the measurement is hard and the incentives are misaligned, this is that. If you want a mature sales process with clear buyer intent and a short cycle, this is not it. But the sector is not going away, and Agreena is one of the few players with enough traction and capital to make it past the pilot phase.

watch_list.why: AI-driven subsurface resource discovery, $115M Series C April 2026, six-plant buildout plan, satellite/remote sensing for conventional geothermal identification
watch_list.roast: You spent a decade at KSAT watching operators drill dry holes in the North Sea because the seismic looked pretty but the reservoir was a lie. Zanskar trained AI on subsurface datasets and satellite observations to find conventional geothermal nobody else saw, then raised $115 million in April to build six plants in the Western US by 2029. It is the same pattern you lived in oil and gas surveillance, finding the signal in noise nobody bothered to process right, except this time the reservoir is heat instead of hydrocarbons and the customer is a hyperscaler buying baseload instead of a super major buying monitoring. They are at the build-things-in-the-ground stage now, which means commercial risk is real and the org will need people who know how to talk to utilities, offtakers, and regulators without the AI pitchbook. You know what happens when the technology works but the commercial infrastructure has not been built yet because you were the one building it for KSAT from 2011 to 2016. Zanskar is pre-revenue but post-doubt, which is the hardest window to hire into because everyone wants proven traction or ground-floor equity and nobody wants the messy middle where you are selling something that exists but has not been sold at scale yet. That is the only window you have ever wanted to work in. If they have a partnerships or commercial role open you should take the call, because the subsurface competence is legitimate and the capital is real and the offtake market exists, which means the only question left is who builds the deals. Check their site.

watch_list.why: Farmer-led reforestation credits with satellite AI MRV, forward carbon contracts, app-based verification, smallholder-focused at scale
watch_list.roast: The carbon credit world has spent five years trying to figure out how to make smallholder agroforestry credits credible without sending an auditor to every ten-hectare plot in Kenya twice a year at a cost that eats the entire margin. Earthbanc built an MRV stack using satellite remote sensing, AI modeling, and a mobile app that farmers actually use to document planting and survival rates, then sells the credits as forward contracts so the finance flows before the trees are twenty years old. It is a harder commercial problem than selling imagery to oil majors because the buyer has to trust a methodology that blends field data with orbital inference, and the seller has to trust that the smallholder in Malawi will keep the trees alive when the incentive payment is two years away. You know that tension because you lived it at GFW, building a commercial licensing program on top of an open-access platform where the fundamental question was always whether the buyer believed the data was worth paying for when a free version existed. Earthbanc has the same structural problem, which is that premium MRV has to justify itself against cheaper practice-based estimates, and the only way to win that argument is to show up with a customer who paid for it and a methodology that held up under scrutiny. They are early, probably ten people, definitely venture-backed, and absolutely in the part of the market where you understand the commercial tension better than most of the people already working there. If they have a partnerships or market development role it is worth a look, because the MRV problem is real and the buyer pool is growing and someone has to build the sales engine that turns the technology into revenue. Check their site.

watch_list.why: Digital MRV for smallholder rice methane reduction using IoT sensors, drones, satellites, blockchain records. India-focused, Fraunhofer partnership, ACRAT project deploying now.
watch_list.roast: Flooded rice paddies produce 25 times more methane per hectare than anyone wants to talk about and CarbonMint built a digital MRV system combining IoT soil sensors, drone spectral imaging, satellite coverage every five days at ten-meter resolution, and blockchain transaction records to prove that farmers in India adopted alternate wetting and drying practices that cut water use and methane emissions without killing yields. It is the kind of system that sounds impossible to operate at scale in fragmented smallholder landscapes until you see that they clustered farmers into cooperatives so the mechanization and digital tools are affordable and the monitoring is distributed across plot boundaries that used to be invisible to any satellite coarser than three meters. The commercial model treats carbon credits as icing, not cake, which means the revenue has to come from input cost savings and yield improvement or the whole thing collapses when carbon prices weaken. You spent two years at GFW trying to convince buyers that the derived product was worth paying for when the base layer was free, and you learned that the only buyers who pay are the ones who need a structured relationship and verified outputs, not just access to the data. CarbonMint has the same dynamic playing out in agricultural carbon, except the verification burden is heavier and the buyer pool is narrower and the regulatory framework is still being written. They are working with Fraunhofer University and deploying in the field right now, which means they are past proof of concept and into the phase where someone has to go build the commercial partnerships with corporates, insurers, and carbon registries. That is where you would add value, because the MRV is credible and the agronomic intervention is proven and the only question left is who will pay for it at scale and how to structure those deals. Check if they have a role open in market development or partnerships, because the problem is real and the solution is being deployed and the commercial function is the missing piece.

watch_list.category: EO / Carbon MRV / AI Foundation Models
watch_list.why: Seed-funded, University of Waterloo origin, building multimodal foundation models trained on satellite remote sensing for carbon removal and nature conservation. Direct analog to Paul's GFW platform-building experience. Backed by HF0, Inovia, Propeller, AWS partnership.
watch_list.roast: Coastal Carbon is building AI foundation models for the natural world, which sounds like the kind of thing a PhD team pitches when they realize memorizing Reddit is not actually useful. They are right. Seed-funded out of Waterloo, backed by people who sold actual companies, working with customers in carbon removal and conservation and trying to make remote sensing data legible to people who are not remote sensing PhDs. The hard part is not the model. The hard part is never the model. The hard part is finding the buyers who will pay before the model is perfect, and then staying close enough to those buyers that you build what they actually need instead of what won a conference poster session. Paul built the commercial licensing program at GFW by first convincing the organization that selling was consistent with the mission. Coastal Carbon will have a version of that fight, because every climate-data startup does. The question is whether they can close the first three deals before the seed round runs out. If they can, they are the kind of company that turns satellite data into revenue instead of into another grant application. If they cannot, they will be extremely well cited.

watch_list.why: Trellis 2026 climate adaptation category finalist. Satellite and sensor-based environmental monitoring, climate risk platforms. Buyers are enterprise and government. Fits Paul's risk-vertical framing (AXA Climate, British Marine) and his comfort selling derived-data products into decision-making workflows.
watch_list.roast: Helix Earth showed up on the Trellis 2026 climate adaptation shortlist, which means they are either solving a real problem or they are very good at filling out applications. Climate adaptation pulled five and a half billion dollars in 2025, up sixty four percent, because everyone finally admitted that we are not preventing the weather and someone needs to sell the forecast. Helix Earth is in that game. Early warning systems, extreme weather forecasting, the tools that help cities and companies figure out whether to sandbag or evacuate. The commercial argument is easier than carbon markets because the buyer does not have to believe in your methodology, they just have to believe it might rain. Paul spent two years convincing Shell that they could not afford to not know where the oil was. Helix Earth has a simpler version of that pitch. The question is whether they have built a product that enterprise risk teams will actually deploy or whether they have built a very beautiful demo that wins awards and dies in procurement. The companies that survive in this space are the ones that close the boring contract with the boring insurance company in month six, not the ones that win the best-in-show at the climate tech summit. If Helix Earth has revenue, Paul should call them. If they do not, he should wait six months and then call them.

watch_list.why: AI platform for natural disaster prep and climate risk reporting. Insurance and enterprise buyers. Growing climate adaptation segment up 64% to $5.5B.
watch_list.roast: Beehive wants to automate your climate risk reporting so you can finally explain to the CFO why the factory flooded again without building a deck from scratch. It is disaster response software written by people who have clearly read the insurance RFPs and understand that nobody actually wants to do scenario modeling by hand. The product is an AI platform that helps companies prepare for wildfires, floods, and the other Greatest Hits of the Anthropocene, which is useful until you remember that half the companies buying this software are preparing for risks they are actively creating. You spent a decade at GFW explaining to people that satellite data tells you what already happened and helps you figure out what to do next. Beehive is selling the same insight to the Fortune 500 except instead of illegal fishing it is their own supply chain on fire. The climate adaptation market is the fastest growing segment in climate tech right now, which tells you everything about how well mitigation is going. The company is early stage, venture backed, classic YC-style build-fast-sell-faster energy. If you can tolerate the irony of selling resilience software to the companies least interested in preventing the problem, this is a decent opener into the insurance and risk analytics verticals where people actually pay for data products instead of just talking about them. You know how to position satellite derived insights. This is that, but the satellite is pointed at the client.

watch_list.why: Mobile lidar and AI for forest measurement. Replaces manual tree surveys. Carbon credit MRV and forestry management buyers.
watch_list.roast: Gaia AI replaces the guy with the calipers and the tape measure, which is a low bar but also the entire business model for half of climate tech. They built a mobile lidar rig that captures 3D point clouds of individual trees while you walk through the forest, then the AI measures trunk diameter, height, and all the other numbers that foresters pretend to care about until it is time to actually cruise a stand. The output is a precise inventory instead of statistical guesswork extrapolated from a few sample plots, which matters a lot if you are trying to sell carbon credits or prove to an investor that the forest you just bought is worth what you said it was. This is MIT bred, venture backed, classic deeptech playbook. You know the routine. The product works, the use case is real, but the commercial question is always whether the customer will pay enough to justify the cost of hardware and the pain of adoption. Forestry moves slow. You spent years at KSAT convincing oil majors that remote sensing was cheaper than waiting for a spill to hit the news. Gaia is selling the same pattern to a different sector, except the buyers have smaller budgets and longer decision cycles. The carbon MRV angle is the wedge. Verification is the only part of the voluntary carbon market where people will actually pay for precision, because the alternative is getting called out by journalists with access to the same satellite data you are pretending to sell. If you can stomach another sector where the revenue model depends on convincing risk averse institutions to adopt new technology before the regulation forces them to, this is that fight. You have had this conversation before. You know how it ends.

watch_list.why: Electrochemical CO2 reformer for steel blast furnace waste gas. Converts emissions to syngas fuel. Steel and heavy industry decarbonization.
watch_list.roast: Helix Carbon is trying to fix steel, which is responsible for seven percent of global emissions and exactly zero percent of the sectors that venture capitalists understand. They built an electrochemical reformer that takes the CO2 coming out of a blast furnace and turns it into syngas that the steel mill can burn as fuel, which is a closed loop that sounds great until you remember that the steel industry has been resisting meaningful change since before your grandparents were born. This is MIT spinout, deep hardware, long development cycles, the kind of company where the first commercial deployment is five years out and the addressable market is every steel mill on earth but the actual market is whoever is desperate enough or subsidized enough to try it first. You know this pattern. You sold to oil majors. Steel is worse. The technology is legitimate. The problem is real. But the path from working prototype to revenue at scale requires navigating procurement bureaucracies, multi year pilot agreements, and plant managers who have been doing it the same way since 1987 and see no reason to stop now. The carbon recycling value prop is compelling in theory, which is exactly the kind of thing that gets you a meetings with sustainability VPs and no follow through from operations. If you want to spend the next three years explaining electrochemistry to people who do not care about electrochemistry but do care about whether this thing will shut down the line, this is the role. You have done harder. Whether you want to do it again in a sector with even less urgency than offshore oil is the real question.

watch_list.why: UK-based, won EUDR compliance contract with ICE, combines LiDAR/SAR with AI for forest carbon verification. Partnership with Upstream Tech. Direct EO-to-carbon-market bridge, exactly Paul's GFW-Planet intersection.
watch_list.roast: Space Intelligence started because two guys got tired of measuring trees by hand in Africa and decided satellites were easier. Fifteen years later they have an EUDR contract with the EU and a partnership with Upstream Tech, which means somebody in Brussels finally figured out that you cannot verify deforestation compliance with a clipboard and a intern. Their tech uses LiDAR, SAR, and custom software to turn public satellite data into something carbon buyers will actually trust, which is the entire game. They are small, UK-based, and selling into the exact regulatory-pressure-creates-demand cycle Paul spent a decade building at KSAT. The founders are academics who figured out how to commercialize, the customer base is split between project developers and regulators, and the product is verification-as-a-service in a market where nobody trusts anything. Paul has spent twenty years on both sides of the satellite data licensing table and built the GFW commercial function on top of an open platform. Space Intelligence is selling the same argument to a different vertical. The difference is that forest carbon has actual money behind it now and EUDR has a compliance deadline. They need somebody who knows how to move a regulator, structure a three-party deal, and explain why high-latency non-commercial MRV does not compete with real-time intelligence. That is the whole Planet Labs negotiation in a different wrapper. If they are hiring for partnerships or commercial, Paul should be on the phone yesterday.

watch_list.why: California-based, built Lens platform for landscape monitoring and MRV. Partnership with Space Intelligence. Geospatial data aggregation and delivery model mirrors GFW's vessel tracking but for land use and carbon. Clear BD/partnerships opportunity.
watch_list.roast: Upstream Tech built Lens, which is a geospatial data platform that makes satellite imagery and land change data actually usable for people who are not GIS nerds. They just partnered with Space Intelligence to put forest MRV data on the platform, which means they figured out that selling access is easier than selling insights. The model is SaaS for climate data, the customer is everyone from NGOs to carbon project developers, and the value proposition is that you do not need a PhD to see what is happening on the ground. Paul built the GFW commercial licensing program by figuring out where derived data has private sector value and then constructing the commercial arguments to get buyers there. Upstream is doing the same thing but for land instead of ocean. They have venture backing, a California base, and a product that lives in the gap between raw satellite feeds and bespoke consulting. The challenge is the same one Paul faced at GFW, which is that a lot of the underlying data is free or cheap and the margin lives in packaging, credibility, and the relationship. They need somebody who has sold complex data products into risk-averse buyers, built partnerships with data providers who might also be competitors, and can translate technical capability into a use case that a procurement officer will actually sign. That is the ASN subsea cable deal and the Planet three-party negotiation in the same conversation. If Upstream is building out partnerships or enterprise sales, Paul is the obvious call.

watch_list.why: India-based but global reach, digital MRV platform for supply chain traceability and carbon verification. Selling into EUDR, ESG reporting, and carbon markets. Combines satellite data with ground truth. Partnerships-heavy model.
watch_list.roast: TraceX is building digital MRV infrastructure for supply chains, which means they are trying to solve the problem of proving that your coffee or your timber or your beef did not come from a place that just got turned into a parking lot. They use satellite data, geospatial intelligence, and blockchain to create audit trails that regulators and buyers will accept, and they are selling into EUDR compliance and ESG reporting mandates, which is regulatory-driven demand at scale. The company is based in India but works globally, the customer base is enterprises who need to prove things, and the model is platform-as-a-service with a partnerships-and-integrations layer on top. Paul spent three years at GFW building commercial licensing on an open data platform and a decade at KSAT figuring out how to sell satellite monitoring into regulatory pressure points. TraceX is the same pattern but in a different sector. The challenge is not the tech, it is figuring out which buyers will pay before the regulation actually lands and building the partnerships with data providers, standards bodies, and implementation partners to make the whole thing work. That is exactly what Paul did with ENI and NOPSEMA in Australia, except this time it is supply chain compliance instead of offshore oil. If they are building out BD or partnerships and need somebody who understands multi-stakeholder deal architecture and how to use pilots to set precedent, Paul wrote that playbook. The only question is whether they want somebody who will tell them the truth about how long these sales cycles actually take.

morning_briefs.notes: Daily / Morning Brief cron commit landed at 12:30 UTC but no morning_briefs entry was appended to dashboard-data.json (existing pipeline bug, last appended entry was 2026-05-01). This run filled the gap. Digest had 276 postings of which ~225 were aggregator-board URLs (Terra.do, Climatebase, Climate Change Jobs by city, Schmidt Marine listings, Esri portfolio, etc.) that link to listing pages rather than specific JDs and are unscoreable per rubric. After applying applications-dedup, watch_list checks, hard exclusions, title-level filtering for engineering/analyst/IC-SDR/recruiter roles, and the recently-ranked window, 8 specific JDs were dispatched to scoring subagents. Subagents could not fetch JDs from their sandboxed environment (WebFetch denied), so fetching was retried in main context. Sierra Club returned 403 (unreadable). 5 successful fetches plus 2 hard-exclusion title-checks plus 1 already-known-pattern (Bentley) produced 4 new Tier 3 dashboard entries: Bees & Bears Head of Risk Berlin (44.5), Bentley Principal Consultant Remote/GA (47.5), SF Estuary Institute Executive Director Richmond CA (52.0), Active Site Chief of Staff Cambridge MA (43.5). No Tier 1 or Tier 2 entries today, no deep dives triggered.

haiku:
Kennedy Center
judge strips Trump's name off the walls
Congress holds the deed

One forty one yes
eight no on climate duty
US votes with Yemen

London hits thirty five
May heat dome traps Europe
the gorge stays April cool

Redhead at Medley
mezcal margs, hockey, then next door
bottle for later

Kachina untracked
May mud season over now
June means hiking boots

Planet three meter
Blue Planet III segment drops
David sees the work

ATS rejection
automatic, no human
haiku hold the line

Rio Grande runoff
Mother's Day races long past
low water by June

Norway in three weeks
Oskar graduates, Vilde waits
Aurlandsdalen hut

Pinyon smoke at dawn
Taos Plaza empty, cool
coffee, then the refresh

The dashboard updates
no ranked opportunities
so you get more poems

Strait of Hormuz shut
Trump weighs Iranian talks
crude futures spike hard

Blue moon in late May
second this month they tell us
once in a blue moon

Europe bakes early
thirty-five point one in May
London sets record

Sergeant down Five-eighteen
motorcycle and pickup truck
Taos mourns the badge

Missing woman found
McGaffey Ridge, Carson Forest
one year of silence

Taos Pueblo cancelled
the powwow this summer gone
no drums in the plaza

Data centers drink
Erin Brockovich maps them all
the grid bends, not breaks

Planet got John Deere
sustainability award
Pelican first light

Red Sox at Cleveland
one forty PM first pitch
summer stretched ahead

Nvidia chip launch
Jensen Huang in Taipei
Intel down six points

Melissa Casias
found in Carson Forest shade
handgun, no answers

Iran talks collapse
Trump tweets, oil spikes, retreats
markets hit records

June first, Taos heat
World Environment Day waits
Azerbaijan host

Sox eight and thirteen
